summary: First-term hospitality-revenue fact pattern. CREW reporting, House Oversight Committee documents, and the underlying foreign Emoluments litigation established the records. Comparative for methodology calibration on what counts as a foreign government official acting in official capacity payment.

primary_sources.label: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington v. Trump, 953 F.3d 178 (2d Cir. 2019). The Second Circuit reinstated the first foreign Emoluments Clause suit, holding the hospitality plaintiffs (CREW, ROC United, Eric Goode, Jill Phaneuf) had competitor standing over foreign-government patronage of Trump properties. S.D.N.Y. No. 1:17-cv-00458 (Daniels, J.), decided September 13, 2019. Rehearing en banc denied, 971 F.3d 102 (2d Cir. 2020), five judges dissenting; vacated as moot, Trump v. CREW, No. 20-330, 141 S. Ct. 1262 (2021). Distinct from SC-004 (D.C. and Maryland) though closely analogous.

primary_sources.organization: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW)
primary_sources.label: CREW, 'Profiting from the Presidency' (year-one report, January 2018). Documented at least 11 foreign governments patronizing Trump businesses and 40 special-interest events at Trump properties in the first year, the Trump International Hotel in Washington prominent among them.

primary_sources.chamber_or_committee: House Committee on Oversight and Accountability (Democratic minority staff)
primary_sources.label: House Oversight Democratic minority staff report, 'White House for Sale: How Princes, Prime Ministers, and Premiers Paid Off President Trump,' January 4, 2024, 156 pages. Found at least $7.8M in payments from 20-plus foreign governments and state-owned entities to four Trump properties (Trump International Hotel Washington, Trump International Hotel Las Vegas, Trump World Tower and Trump Tower New York) over 2017-2018, drawn from Mazars USA records obtained through litigation; China the largest source at roughly $5.6M. Ranking Member Raskin foreword.

summary: Tests the state-grant-as-payment framing. Trademarks were awarded by a Chinese state IP office on a non-routine timeline coinciding with her White House role. Comparative reference for whether non-cash sovereign benefits register as foreign Emoluments Clause flows.

primary_sources.registry: China Trademark Office, State Administration for Industry and Commerce (SAIC); trademark functions reorganized into the China National Intellectual Property Administration (CNIPA) in 2018
primary_sources.label: China Trademark Office grants to Ivanka Trump Marks LLC, 2017-2018. Grant clusters coincided with administration policy events: provisional approvals around April 6, 2017 (the day Ivanka Trump and Jared Kushner dined with Xi Jinping at Mar-a-Lago); registration and first-trial approvals in May and June 2018 around the ZTE sanctions reversal and a contemporaneous reported $500M Chinese loan to a Trump Organization-linked Indonesia project; and 18 further marks granted to Trump and Ivanka-linked companies over October to November 2018, bringing the year total to roughly 34. Applicant Ivanka Trump Marks LLC. Individual registration numbers are held in the registry database but were not published in the source reporting (disclosed gap).

Associated Press reporting on the China trademark grants to Ivanka Trump Marks LLC (Erika Kinetz), 2017-2019, establishing the counts and the timing relative to trade negotiations and the ZTE reversal; the May 2018 cluster reported May 28, 2018 and the 18-marks-in-two-months tally reported November 2018.

primary_sources.organization: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW)
primary_sources.label: CREW trademark-database reviews flagging the timing of the China grants relative to Trump administration China policy, 2018; the May 2018 registration approvals coinciding with the ZTE announcement noted as the worked example.

summary: Anchor case for the foreign Emoluments Clause modern doctrinal posture. Reached the Fourth Circuit on standing and zone-of-interests; vacated and remanded as moot in January 2021 after Trump left office. The case law on emolument definition still governs the framework this tracker uses.

primary_sources.label: In re Trump (DC and Maryland v. Trump), 958 F.3d 274 (4th Cir. 2020) (en banc). Full court denied the President's petition for a writ of mandamus and allowed the foreign Emoluments Clause claims to proceed. D. Md. No. 8:17-cv-01596 (Messitte, J.), decided May 14, 2020.

Trump v. District of Columbia, No. 20-331, 141 S. Ct. 1262 (2021) (mem.). Cert granted, judgment vacated, case remanded to the Fourth Circuit with instructions to dismiss as moot under Munsingwear, January 25, 2021. Fourth Circuit vacatur 838 F. App'x 789 (4th Cir. 2021); D. Md. case closed May 11, 2021.

summary: DC Circuit held individual members of Congress lacked standing to sue over foreign Emoluments Clause violations absent institutional authorization. Load-bearing for any future enforcement challenge.

primary_sources.label: Blumenthal v. Trump, 949 F.3d 14 (D.C. Cir. 2020) (per curiam). The D.C. Circuit held that individual members of Congress lack standing to sue over the Foreign Emoluments Clause absent institutional authorization. No. 19-5237, decided February 7, 2020; cert denied, 141 S. Ct. 553 (2020). D.D.C. No. 1:17-cv-01154 (Sullivan, J.). Not vacated in the 2021 moot-vacatur wave; remains good law on legislative standing.

summary: Documented for methodology completeness as the case the framework excludes despite political salience. Burisma is a Cyprus-registered private holding company; no Ukrainian state ownership in the documented record. State ties run through the owner's prior ministerial tenure, not through ownership structure. The exclusion is symmetrical: a Republican-side analogue with the same facts would also fall outside scope.
documented_amount: ~$3.4M to Rosemont Seneca Bohai LLC (entity-level inflows)

primary_sources.registry: Cyprus Department of Registrar of Companies and Official Receiver (DRCOR)
primary_sources.label: Burisma Holdings Limited, Cyprus registration HE186236, Department of Registrar of Companies and Official Receiver. Incorporated as a Cyprus private limited company on October 26, 2006; registered office in Limassol; status dissolution following voluntary liquidation, dissolved February 16, 2023. Owned by Brociti Investments Limited (Mykola Zlochevsky). The registry record shows private ownership with no Ukrainian state shareholding, the documentary basis for the out-of-scope classification.

primary_sources.chamber_or_committee: Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs and Senate Committee on Finance (majority staff, joint)
primary_sources.label: Senate HSGAC and Finance majority staff joint report, 'Hunter Biden, Burisma, and Corruption: The Impact on U.S. Government Policy and Related Concerns,' September 23, 2020. Characterizes the Burisma board compensation and related financial flows. Cited for the documented-record basis of the case, not for a sovereign nexus, which the report does not establish.

Reuters review of bank records reporting that Burisma sent approximately $3.4 million to Rosemont Seneca Bohai LLC between April 2014 and November 2015, the entity-level inflow behind the documented_amount. Reuters noted it could not independently verify the documents or how much Hunter Biden personally received.

summary: Decentralized finance protocol and stablecoin issuer founded 2024 by Zachary Folkman, Chase Herro, the Witkoff family (Alex and Zach Witkoff), and Trump family members (Donald Trump Jr., Eric Trump, Barron Trump). Donald Trump and Steve Witkoff are listed in WLF corporate documentation as co-founders emeritus. Trump family entities receive 75% of net WLFI token-sale proceeds plus a cut of stablecoin profits. Three sovereign-adjacent transactions sit on the record. On January 16, 2025, four days before inauguration, Aryam (a vehicle identified in WSJ reporting as controlled by lieutenants of Sheikh Tahnoon bin Zayed Al Nahyan, UAE National Security Advisor and chair of ADQ, IHC, G42, and MGX) acquired 49% of WLF for $500M, with $187M flowing to Trump family entities and $31M flowing to Witkoff-associated entities at signing. The transaction was not publicly disclosed for over a year. On May 1, 2025, MGX used WLF's USD1 stablecoin to settle a $2B Binance investment. MGX is an Abu Dhabi technology-investment vehicle whose two founding and core shareholders are Mubadala (a sovereign wealth fund wholly owned by the Government of Abu Dhabi) and G42 (an Emirati AI holding company chaired by Sheikh Tahnoun bin Zayed Al Nahyan of the ruling family), and it is overseen by Tahnoun bin Zayed (UAE national security advisor and brother of the president). On January 14, 2026, the Pakistan Virtual Asset Regulatory Authority (PVARA) and the Pakistan Ministry of Finance signed a non-binding MOU with SC Financial Technologies (a Delaware-registered WLF affiliate that co-owns the USD1 stablecoin brand) to explore integrating USD1 into Pakistan's regulated cross-border payments framework. Pakistani Finance Minister Muhammad Aurangzeb signed for Pakistan; Zach Witkoff (CEO of both WLF and SC Financial Technologies) signed for the US side. The signing ceremony was attended by Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif and Field Marshal Asim Munir. The MOU is non-binding and exploratory; no Pakistani-sovereign payment to SC Financial Technologies or WLF is documented as of this entry. The Tahnoon-Aryam transaction carries the v2.0 convergent-interest flag for Witkoff Group and WLF portfolio overlap; Steve Witkoff served as Middle East envoy from early 2025, with continuing equity exposures across Witkoff Group affiliates and WLF-related vehicles per his August 13, 2025 OGE Form 278e (New Entrant Report), which lists both World Liberty Financial (entry 41.8.1) and SC Financial Technologies (entry 41.9.1) as retained holdings under Witkoff Holdings LLC, the two entities that own and control the USD1 brand and its associated trademarks. USD1 itself is issued, minted, redeemed, and custodied by BitGo (BitGo Bank & Trust, N.A., an OCC-regulated national trust bank), not by WLF; brand ownership and issuance are distinct roles. Per BitGo's monthly USD1 Reserve Reports, total USD1 redeemable tokens outstanding rose from $2.73B (December 9, 2025) to a peak of $5.14B (February 17, 2026) before settling at $4.39B (March 31, 2026), indicating the scale of the brand the Witkoff-held entities control; this is a structural figure and implies no causal link to any official act. Pre-v2.0 records are not re-examined; this is the worked v1.x case the v2.0 flag was developed against.
documented_amount: $500M Aryam stake (Tahnoon, Jan 2025); $2B MGX-Binance via USD1 (May 2025)

primary_sources.label: Witkoff OGE Form 278e (New Entrant Report, August 13, 2025)
WLF corporate documentation (capital structure, founders, profit-distribution terms)

primary_sources.chamber_or_committee: House Select Committee on the CCP

Warren-Murphy letter on Witkoff OGE form ethics-officer signoff, November 2025
NYT, 'Where Mideast Envoy Pitched Peace, His Son Pitched Investors' (Kamin & Hope, September 26, 2025; corrected October 5, 2025)
WSJ, 'Spy Sheikh Secret Stake Trump Crypto Tahnoon' series, January 31, 2026

Warren-Merkley letter to the MGX and Binance CEOs (Senate Banking Committee, June 11, 2025), reporting WLF's confirmation the transaction would likely have settled in foreign fiat absent USD1
USD1 stablecoin reserve documentation identifying World Liberty Financial, Inc. and SC Financial Technologies, LLC as owners/controllers of the USD1 brand (July 2025)

BitGo USD1 Reserve Reports (monthly attestation packages, December 2025–March 2026; stored at docs/sources/usd1-attestations/), issuer's published representation, p.2 of each package: 'the USD1 brand and certain associated trademarks are owned and controlled by World Liberty Financial, Inc. and SC Financial Technologies, LLC,' while USD1 'is issued and redeemed by [BitGo].' The brand-ownership sentence sits in the USD1 Reserve Report portion, footnoted as not subject to Crowe LLP's Independent Accountant's Examination, an issuer representation, not auditor-attested. The reserve figures in the same packages are Crowe-examined.
Mubadala official announcement co-announcing the creation of MGX by the AIATC, naming Mubadala and G42 as founding partners (September 2024), primary anchor for MGX's two-shareholder ownership

family_member: Steve Witkoff (Special Envoy for the Middle East; covered intermediary under v2.0)
summary: v2.0 covered-intermediary worked case. On February 19, 2026, the Pakistani Finance Ministry and the US General Services Administration signed a non-binding MOU to jointly redevelop the Roosevelt Hotel, a 22-story 1,025-room property in midtown Manhattan owned by Pakistan International Airlines. Per the Pakistani government statement, the agreement was 'negotiated and stewarded' by US Special Envoy for the Middle East Steve Witkoff. Pakistani officials estimate the asset value at over $1 billion. Under the proposed structure, Pakistan contributes the land while a development partner puts up roughly $1B equity and the joint venture takes on $2-3B in debt. PIA was privatized in December 2025 as part of Pakistan's IMF Extended Fund Facility restructuring; the Pakistani government retains a minority stake. Witkoff is the founder of Witkoff Group, a New York real estate firm whose Manhattan portfolio includes the Times Square Edition and the Faena, both luxury redevelopment projects in the same market segment as the Roosevelt. NY Post and Reuters reporting describes Witkoff as having potential post-diplomatic interest in Roosevelt-related deals; this is reporting speculation, not a contractual position. Witkoff Group is not a party to the MOU, and as of this entry no PIA-source flow to Witkoff Group is documented. The convergent interest is at the role-and-portfolio level: covered intermediary (envoy with foreign-policy portfolio overlapping the deal counterparty), Manhattan real estate firm whose sector aligns with the redevelopment plan, sovereign-adjacent counterparty (post-privatization PIA with Pakistani government minority stake). Witkoff's continuing equity exposures across Witkoff Group affiliates are documented on the August 13, 2025 OGE Form 278e referenced in SC-007. The record will be updated if Witkoff Group becomes a contractual party or a PIA-source flow is documented.
documented_amount: $1B+ asset value (estimated by Pakistan); no documented Witkoff Group receipt as of February 2026

primary_sources.label: Pakistan Finance Division statement on Roosevelt Hotel MOU, February 19, 2026
Witkoff OGE Form 278e (New Entrant Report, August 13, 2025)

Reuters reporting on the Roosevelt Hotel MOU and Pakistan IMF context, February 2026
Commercial Observer, 'Trump Envoy Steve Witkoff, Pakistani Government Plan Roosevelt Hotel Redevelopment', February 26, 2026
Bisnow New York, 'Steve Witkoff Strikes U.S.-Pakistan Deal To Redevelop Manhattan Hotel', February 20, 2026

headline_exclusion_reason: Logged as a v2.0 covered-intermediary watch entry. As of February 2026, Witkoff Group is not a party to the Roosevelt Hotel MOU and no PIA-source flow to a Witkoff entity is documented; post-privatization PIA Investments carries only a minority Pakistani-government stake. Promotes to a headline record if a PIA-source flow to a Witkoff entity, or a Witkoff Group contractual position in the redevelopment, is documented.

summary: First adv-sourced LIVE record: the sovereign-source nexus lives in SEC Form ADV, not an OGE Form 278e. A Fin Management LLC ('Affinity Partners') is a Delaware LLC formed in 2021, headquartered in Sunny Isles Beach, Florida, and wholly owned by Jared Kushner (ADV Part 2A, Item 4.A). As of December 31, 2025 it manages $6,160,297,411 in regulatory assets, all discretionary (Item 4.E), under a 2.0% annualized management fee plus performance carry of up to 20% of realized proceeds (Items 5.A, 6). On roughly $6.16B the management fee alone runs on the order of $120M per year before carry. In its own ADV, Affinity discloses that the investor categories for its private funds include 'sovereign wealth funds' (Item 7); this is the sovereign-source nexus confirmed in the primary record itself. The Part 1A Schedule D breakdown (2026-03 annual update) lists six private funds, with Affinity Partners Parallel Fund I LP holding about 70% of AUM ($4,307,145,842) across only six beneficial owners, a concentration consistent with large sovereign commitments rather than a broad limited-partner base. The specific sovereign funds are named only in secondary reporting, held on separate evidentiary footing: a roughly $2B Public Investment Fund (Saudi) anchor commitment in 2021, which PIF's own screening panel recommended rejecting before Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman, who chairs PIF's board, overruled it (House Oversight 2022; Senate Finance / Wyden 2022–2024; NYT), and approximately $1.5B combined from the Qatar Investment Authority and Abu Dhabi's Lunate in 2024 (Reuters; Kushner's own remarks). The ADV confirms sovereign-wealth-fund capital is present by category; it does not itself name PIF, QIA, or Lunate, which are three distinct sovereign / sovereign-adjacent vehicles cited individually and never fused as 'Gulf money'. The record documents a financial relationship at scale (billions in sovereign-source capital placed in a vehicle wholly owned by a former senior official, generating fee income to that official) and makes no causal claim. The oversight framing of the PIF commitment as payback for Kushner's official acts is a payment-caused-outcome inference and is not adopted: Kushner held no office during the reporting period (he left government in January 2021 and Affinity was incorporated the day after), so there is no contemporaneous official act to link, which is precisely why the relationship stands documented without a causal hook. Affinity's participation in the 2025–26 Electronic Arts acquisition consortium (PIF-controlled, with Silver Lake), in which Kushner reportedly introduced PIF to EA, is noted as business development only; he held no office in 2025–26 and no official act attaches. Promoted from watchlist W-002.
documented_amount: AUM $6,160,297,411 @ 2025-12-31 (2% management fee + up to 20% carry); named sovereign commitments ~$3.5B (PIF ~$2B, 2021; QIA + Lunate ~$1.5B, 2024)

primary_sources.label: SEC Form ADV, A Fin Management LLC ('Affinity Partners'), firm CRD 315482, Part 2A brochure dated 2026-03-22: Item 4.A sole owner Jared Kushner (Delaware LLC, 2021); Item 4.E regulatory AUM $6,160,297,411 @ 2025-12-31 (all discretionary); Items 5.A/6 2% management fee + up to 20% carry; Item 7 private-fund investor categories include 'sovereign wealth funds'

SEC Form ADV Part 1A Schedule D (2026-03 annual update), A Fin Management LLC, six private funds with regulatory AUM and beneficial-owner counts; Affinity Partners Parallel Fund I LP $4,307,145,842 across 6 beneficial owners

primary_sources.chamber_or_committee: House Committee on Oversight and Reform
primary_sources.label: House Oversight Chairwoman Carolyn Maloney, letter to Jared Kushner opening the committee probe of the Saudi PIF investment in A Fin Management LLC (Affinity), June 2, 2022. States the $2B PIF raise and Affinity's filing-indicated roughly $25M per year in management fees, and notes Affinity was incorporated in Delaware on January 21, 2021, the day after the first administration ended.
Senate Finance Committee correspondence on PIF–Affinity Partners (Wyden investigation, 2022–2024)

David D. Kirkpatrick and Kate Kelly, 'Before Giving Billions to Jared Kushner, Saudi Investment Fund Had Big Doubts,' The New York Times, April 10, 2022. Documents-based account of the PIF screening panel recommending rejection (firm inexperience, an asset-management fee it called excessive, public-relations risk from Kushner's prior White House role) and the full PIF board under Mohammed bin Salman overruling it days later.

Iain Withers, 'Kushner's Affinity's assets jump to $4.8 billion after Gulf cash injection,' Reuters, January 2025. Reports the $1.5B 2024 commitment from the Qatar Investment Authority and Abu Dhabi's Lunate, per Kushner's December 2024 podcast remarks and the A Fin Management ADV. Corroborated by Bloomberg, 'Kushner's Affinity Gets Additional $1.5 Billion From Qatar, Abu Dhabi's Lunate,' December 20, 2024.
